title: Dissipation-coherence tradeoff for stochastic oscillations
abstract: Autonomous noisy oscillations in biochemical and mesoscopic systems require nonequilibrium driving and therefore dissipation. A striking conjecture by Oberreiter, Barato, and Seifert (OBS) proposes a universal lower bound on the entropy produced per oscillation period in terms of the coherence number of the slowest oscillatory mode. Here we derive a weaker but rigorous lower bound that preserves the OBS structure while introducing a mode-uniformity factor that quantifies how evenly the oscillatory eigenmode is distributed across states in the steady-state inner product. The result makes explicit that an eigenvalue-only prefactor can fail when the dominant oscillatory mode is localized. We also outline a proof-of-principle route for estimating this factor from low-dimensional data under single-mode dominance and sufficiently informative measurements, and derive an eigenvector-free corollary using only the smallest stationary probability. Translation-invariant Markov jump processes on a ring provide a symmetry-protected class with $η=1$, so the refinement reduces to the OBS form; the drift--diffusion limit on a circle saturates the bound.
summary: This paper refines the Oberreiter-Barato-Seifert conjecture by introducing a mode-uniformity factor, $\eta$, into the lower bound on entropy production during stochastic oscillations. This factor quantifies how localized the dominant oscillatory eigenmode is within the system's state space. The work provides both theoretical derivations and practical, data-driven recipes to estimate this crucial parameter from limited experimental measurements.
main_problem: Deriving a rigorous lower bound on the entropy produced per oscillation period for noisy, non-equilibrium systems that refines existing conjectures by accounting for mode localization.
main_result: The paper establishes $\Delta S \geq 4\pi^2 \eta N$, where $\eta$ is a mode-uniformity factor quantifying how evenly the oscillatory eigenmode is distributed across states. This shows that dissipation can be parametrically smaller if the dominant mode is localized ($\eta \ll 1$).
method: The analysis uses Markov jump processes and spectral properties of the generator matrix, introducing $\eta$ to correct the standard eigenvalue-based bound.
model_or_system: The study models stochastic oscillations using finite irreducible continuous-time Markov jump processes on discrete state spaces (e.g., a ring or general graph). The dynamics are governed by a Master Equation ($\dot{P}(t) = W P(t)$).
key_observables: Steady-state entropy production rate ($\sigma$), coherence number ($N$), and the mode-uniformity factor ($\eta$).
important_parameters: The ratio $\eta$ controls the sharpness of the dissipation-coherence tradeoff; the bound reduces to the original conjecture when $\eta=1$ (delocalized modes).
main_assumptions: The rigorous derivation relies on algebraic inequalities related to the Dirichlet form, while estimation methods are heuristic and assume single-mode dominance.
figures_summary: Figure 1 plots a tightness ratio $Y = \Delta S / (4\pi^2 N)$ versus $\eta$, demonstrating that the measured ratio satisfies $Y \geq \eta$ across various model ensembles.
paper_structure: The paper first derives the rigorous inequality incorporating $\eta$. It then outlines proof-of-principle routes for estimating $\eta$ from low-dimensional data (DMD/Koopman methods) and presents an eigenvector-free corollary based on the smallest stationary probability.
why_interesting: It provides a quantitative, state-dependent correction ($\eta$) to fundamental bounds in non-equilibrium statistical mechanics that govern energy dissipation during coherent oscillations, which is crucial for understanding open quantum systems dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Numerical verification of the theorem bound Y = σλR/λ2 I ≥η across model ensembles. Hollow markers are samples from the ensembles described in Sec. II B generated with k+ = 1 and k−= 0.5: translation-invariant rings with n ∈{10, 20, 50, 100}; heterogeneous rings with n = 50 and disorder strength s ∈{0.05, 0.10, 0.20, 0.30} (40 samples per s); and OBS-style fully connected models with n = 40, all- to-all link rate ε ∈{10−4, 3 × 10−4}, and outgoing disorder sout ∈{0.5, 1.0, 1.5} (25 samples per (ε, sout)). Only samples with coherence number N ≥0.05 are shown. The diagonal line shows Y = η.
